column4: Hypertension Management In Primary Care: Reach, Adoption, And Demographic Distribution Of Remote Patient Monitoring
column5:
Introduction
Remote patient monitoring (RPM) is a telehealth technology to monitor and transmit health data using electronic devices to facilitate management of chronic conditions such as hypertension. Information specific to patients’ sociodemographic characteristics related to RPM in primary care setting is scarce. The study objective was to report reach and adoption of RPM for managing hypertension in the primary care setting of University of Alabama at Birmingham (UAB) health system.
Methods
It was a cross sectional study for the period between June 2020 and May 2022. Data from UAB Informatics for Integrating Biology and the Bedside (i2b2) and Health System Information Services (HSIS) were merged to estimate reach and adoption of RPM. we reported descriptive statistics and likelihood of RPM enrollment using log-binomial model.
Results/Discussion
Out of 2339 patients, 331 (14.15%) enrolled in RPM, and 35 (59.32%) PCPs made RPM enrollments. RPM enrolled patients were on average, aged 64.82 (±14.17) years, with 61.93% female, 61.33% Black and 96.07% from metropolitan residence, whereas average age for UC was 59.68 (±13.36) years, with 50.30% female, 58.12% Black and 92.58% from metropolitan residence. Crude estimate from log binomial model indicated that patients living in metropolitan area were more likely (RR: 1.92, p=0.02) to enroll in RPM. In the adjusted model, age (RR 1.03, p<0.001) and sex (RR (male): 0.69, p<0.001) were significant predictors for RPM enrollment.
Significance
This study highlights the importance of qualitative exploration of barriers and facilitators for RPM reach and adoption for managing hypertension in the primary care setting.
column6: This study explored the sociodemographic patient factors related to RPM enrollment for hypertension management. Identifying sociodemographic predictors of RPM enrollment can play crucial role in healthcare policy making to ensure equity in hypertension management using telehealth technology.

column4: Developing a STEM Resilience Curriculum Using High Impact Teaching Practices
column5: Undergraduate students (UGs), particularly Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) and pre-healthcare UGs, are under high levels of stress. Further, stress among those from disadvantage backgrounds, such as minority students and those from disadvantaged socioeconomic backgrounds also have a compounded feeling of stress as their counterparts, especially at Predominantly White Institutions (PWIs). Furthermore, burnout among STEM and healthcare professionals is at an all-time high, causing decreased retention in several fields. There are increased efforts on combating this burnout and increasing support at the professional level, but this may be too late. Therefore, it is essential that training in resilience and stress management is provided to pre-health professionals and STEM students before they enter the workforce. We propose a curriculum that instructs our undergraduate health professional students on understanding the connection between biological and psychological on stress while simultaneously identifying their stressors and learning ways to manage their own stress that is rooted in self-efficacy theory. To accomplish this, we have designed a curriculum that contains two courses, an introductory course focused on The Effects of Physiological and Psychological Stress and stress management and a Course Based Undergraduate Research Experience (CURE) focused on analyzing previously collected UG student stress data. This curriculum will help students to increase their resilience and increase retention for STEM and healthcare professionals.
column6: To improve self-efficacy in STEM and pre-healthcare UGs allowing them to maintain over mental well-being and prevent burnout.

column4: Bringing Cancer Clinical Trials to the Medically Underserved
column5:
INTRODUCTION: This study investigates referral mechanisms from community-based oncology care (Cooper Green) to O’Neal Comprehensive Cancer Center (OCCC), assesses minority groups’ clinical trial knowledge, and explores their perceptions and attitudes toward trials. 

METHODS A sequential explanatory mixed methods approach was employed to evaluate patient knowledge, perspectives, and factors influencing the referral process for cancer clinical trials. The Clinical Trial Knowledge survey was administered to 97 Cooper Green patients. Demographics and survey responses were analyzed descriptively, and multivariate analysis was conducted using STATA 18. Qualitative data from seven semi-structured interviews were analyzed using NVivo, with themes identified through deductive and inductive methods.

RESULTS: Five hundred-two mailers were sent, yielding a 9.56% response rate. The 97 participants had an average age of 54 and were predominantly single, Black, or African American. 61.86% of respondents agreed that patient participation is crucial for generating new and effective treatments, and 59.79% preferred receiving extensive written information before consenting to participate - however, 32.99% perceived clinical trial participation as frightening. Four main themes related to clinical trials were identified. Participants varied in their engagement with their health. Although many had limited knowledge of clinical trials, they were strongly willing to participate. The decision-making process was complex, influenced by awareness and attitudes towards clinical trials.

DISCUSSION: Findings emphasize the critical role of awareness and education in addressing underrepresentation in clinical trials. Tailored, culturally congruent interventions are essential to bridge the knowledge gap and enhance participation rates among racial and ethnic minorities.
column6:
This study highlights the challenges and opportunities in increasing clinical trial enrollment among racial and ethnic minority patients, underscoring the need for targeted interventions to address underrepresentation and enhance participation rates.
   
The findings underscore the importance of raising awareness and providing culturally sensitive education to overcome barriers to clinical trial participation, ultimately contributing to more inclusive and effective cancer research and treatment strategies.

column4: Area Bias and the Adoption of Inclusion Strategy in U.S. Hospitals
column5:
Introduction: The healthcare sector plays a pivotal role in promoting the well-being of diverse populations. However, persistent disparities highlight the urgent need for comprehensive strategy initiatives that prioritize Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) and address implicit bias within the healthcare system. Inclusion strategy is defined by the adoption of DEI policies and procedures to foster an inclusive environment for patients and staff. Area bias is a measure of the average implicit bias of the population in which the hospital serves. Implicit bias can impede the work of diversity management strategy initiative adoption in our healthcare system given the subconscious prejudices that healthcare professionals may unknowingly hold. 
Methods: This cross-sectional study analyzes a nationally representative sample of 78 hospitals and healthcare organizations, examining community bias scores based on geographic FIPS codes. The dataset was compounded to include the presence of explicit DEI priorities of those same organizations, considering variables such as gender and ethnic diversity in leadership, explicit diversity language in mission/vision/values, and the presence of diversity statements. 
Results: Findings will indicate the associations between diversity hospital strategy management and area biases. 
Implications: Results of this study have important implications with regard to racism in healthcare and health equity. The study emphasizes the potential influence of diversity management strategy not only with regard to healthcare delivery to patients but also recruitment of professionals. This study also highlights a subset of organizations that should prioritize DEI strategy given pervasive bias that exists outside the walls of the institution. If not, a psychologically safe and inclusive organizational culture could remain stagnant and its potential influence on health equity could be detrimental.
Conclusion: Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) have become central to corporate practices, especially in the healthcare industry. Organizations in communities with high bias scores who actively communicate their commitment to DEI through explicit language and diverse leadership may be better positioned to best care for diverse communities and to attract the best talent.
Keywords: Implicit bias, psychological safety, DEI, hospitals, strategy
column6: Organizations in communities with high bias scores who actively communicate their commitment to DEI through explicit language and diverse leadership may be better positioned to best care for diverse communities and to attract the best talent.

column4: Perceptions of Healthcare Students toward Pulmonary Rehabilitation Programs for COPD Patients
column5: Introduction: Assessing students' perceptions is crucial to improve COPD management and determine the need for educational courses regarding pulmonary rehabilitation (PR) programs. PR is an effective non-pharmacological intervention for COPD patients. It aims to improve lung function, exercise capacity, and quality of life. The purpose of this study was to assess the perceptions of healthcare students regarding PR programs. Methods: Students were enrolled at a large urban university in the southeastern United States. The data used were collected from three different healthcare programs at the university. Data were collected through a self-administered survey consisting of 38 questions on a 5-point Likert-type scale. The survey evaluated student perceptions and was examined for face validity by respiratory therapy educators at the university. The data were analyzed using the Statistical Package for the Social Sciences. Results: A total of 178 students completed the survey, including respiratory therapy (43.3%), physical therapy (39.9%), and occupational therapy (16.9%). The study findings revealed there were differences in the perceptions of students regarding PR. There was a significant difference in positive perceptions between students who had experience attending a PR program and those who did not (p = 0.043). There were no differences found among students in their familiarity with PR programs (p = 0.060). Finally, there were no differences in the perception of students regarding the factors that influence COPD patients' decisions not to participate in the PR programs. Significance: This study provides preliminary information for developing a PR education course for the curriculum of healthcare programs.
column6: This study provides preliminary information for developing a PR education course for the curriculum of healthcare programs.

column4: Comparative Analysis of Protocolized versus Non-protocolized Weaning from Mechanical Ventilation in ARDS Patients Using Electrical Impedance Tomography
column5: Background: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is characterized by an acute, diffuse, inflammatory lung injury, leading to increased alveolar capillary permeability, and loss of aerated lung tissue. ARDS is life-threatening and effects approximately 200,000 patients annually in the United States, resulting in nearly 75,000 deaths, more than breast cancer or HIV infection. Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT) is a non-invasive imaging technique with the potential for monitoring and evaluating lung function in critically ill patients, including those diagnosed with ARDS. The purpose of this study is to perform a scoping review on the use of EIT in adult patients undergoing mechanical ventilation. Methods: This review will be conducted following the methodological framework of Arksey and O'Malley. The data will be extracted from  MEDLINE, EMBASE, CINAHL, Cochrane, and the Web of Science. The inclusion criteria will screen randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and will encompass articles that employ any type of EIT. We will only consider articles that are written exclusively in English. [Include more information regarding your inclusion criteria e.g., dates of publication, population of interest, etc.] Results: We will compare protocolized versus non-protocolized weaning of patients undergoing mechanical ventilation using EIT in terms of healthcare resource utilization, including ventilator days, ICU length of stay, and hospital costs. Significance: This review will characterize the utilization of EIT in optimizing weaning protocols and enhancing patient outcomes during the process of weaning from mechanical ventilation in ARDS patients.
column6:
•This review will provide valuable insight into the efficacy of various weaning strategies and offer a deeper understanding of the physiological mechanisms and regional ventilation patterns associated with successful weaning. 
•With greater understanding, patient care in critical care environments will be improved with development of individualized and evidence-based weaning methods.

column4: Implementation of AI in Healthcare Supply Chain Management
column5:
Introduction: 
In response to the complicated challenges faced by healthcare supply chain management (SCM), and an increasing demand in utilization of SCM in healthcare domain in recent years, the adoption of advanced technologies involving Artificial Intelligence (AI) has increased. AI based tools and technologies that offer data-driven insights and predictive analytics hold promise in revolutionizing healthcare supply chain operations by (1) optimizing inventory control, (2) enhancing demand forecasting, and (3) streamlining distribution logistics. The objective of this 
systematic literature review is to provide a comprehensive analysis of the potential contextual factors influencing AI adoption in healthcare SCM. We utilize the RE-AIM framework [1] for SCM and operations management for this analysis. The findings of our study highlight trends, challenges & opportunities, and contribute to a clear understanding of how AI can enhance healthcare logistics and utilization of SCM in healthcare. This review also offers healthcare SCM professionals who are aiming to leverage AI for improved efficiency and accuracy with practical insights and provide future research directions. Ultimately, this paper advocates for the strategic adoption of AI in healthcare SCM to address thus identified dynamic challenges and complexities inherent in the delivery of healthcare services, 
contributing to enhanced operational efficiency and patient care outcomes for value-based care.
Methods: 
We conduct a literature search in PubMed and Scopus databases using combinations of keywords in three sets: (1) Technology relating to "machine learning”, “transfer learning" ,"deep learning", "support vector machine", 
(2) Contextual action domain – “implementation” or “implementation science”, and lastly, (3) Health domain-“healthcare-operation”, “healthcare management”, “healthcare supply chain” . We utilize the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) framework [2]. The search has initially resulted in a total of 2280 publications. In next steps, we will screen these publications using inclusion and exclusion criteria. The inclusion criteria contain studies that focus on AI applications in healthcare supply chain management, published in English. We use the RE-AIM framework that facilitates a systematic analysis of each publication across dimensions of Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, and Maintenance. It allows categorization of included 
publications on critical dimensions and helps gain insights into the practical implications of AI interventions in healthcare supply chains.
Results: 
The results of this review reveal an understanding of the current landscape of Artificial Intelligence (AI) implementation in healthcare supply chain management (SCM). Key findings from analysis of included publications highlight the successes, challenges, and critical success factors influencing the adoption of AI in healthcare domain.The literature review publications highlight how AI interventions positively impact healthcare supply chain outcomes through optimizing inventory control, demand forecasting, and distribution logistics, as well as, providing tangible improvements in transparency and efficiency. Factors such as technological sophistication, data quality, government support, and interoperability which are derived from the analyzed publications are identified as critical success factors. Possible future research should explore the integration of emerging technologies alongside AI. Investigating how blockchain, the Internet of Things (IoT), or other advanced analytics methods complement AI in healthcare SCM will contribute to a more holistic understanding. Concerning to the implementation science aspect, examining successful case studies and strategies for overcoming challenges in AI adoption can provide practical guidance for healthcare supply chain managers.
column6:
The systematic literature review highlights how AI can be applied in healthcare supply chain management. The successes outlined in the literature, coupled with identified challenges and critical success factors, create a roadmap for organizations looking to leverage AI for improved efficiency and accuracy in their supply chain operations. The findings contribute significantly to the existing body of knowledge and call for continued research and practical adoption of AI in healthcare SCM. As this review paper navigates the complexities of healthcare 
logistics, the integration of AI stands as a promising path for enhancing overall supply chain performance and, ultimately, improving patient outcomes for value-based care.

column4: Predictors of Belonging among Clinical Support Staff: A Mixed Methods Study
column5:
Introduction: Amidst ongoing human capital challenges in healthcare, promoting belonging is one way to improve the psychological wellbeing and retention of healthcare workers.  The purpose of this study is to examine predictors of belonging among clinical support staff (CSS).  

Methods: In 2022, 985 CSS completed an engagement survey at an academic medical center in the Southeastern U.S. We used a concurrent mixed methods approach. Logistic regression examined the association of job demands and resources with belonging.  The qualitative strand employed emergent coding of an open-ended response describing organizational culture.

Results/Discussion: Job satisfaction, psychological safety, perceived organizational support, recognition, respect, and workload were statistically significant predictors of belonging.  Qualitative analysis added nuance to why recognition and respect impacted belonging, and contradicted findings on workload. Emergent themes supported good working relationships, diversity, morale, quality, staffing, and professional role as additional contributors. 
Overall, findings support five previously-suggested strategies for enhancing belonging (1) valuing employees’ contributions (2) promoting connection with coworkers (3) supporting workers in daily and long-term work goals, (4) having organizational values and purposes that inspire worker pride, and (5) job satisfaction.  Qualitative findings suggest ways that organizations may address these for CSS, such as changing the nurse/physician-centric culture and better integrating CSS into interdisciplinary work.

Significance: Following nurses, CSS is the second largest group of healthcare workers in the U.S.. Retaining these workers is critical for healthcare organizations; yet predictors of belonging among CSS has not been studied. Findings can inform a targeted approach to improve belonging and retention of CSS.
column6:
- To our knowledge, this is the first study of predictors of belonging among clinical support staff, the second largest group of healthcare workers in the United States (second only to nurses).
- The mixed-methods approach utilized in this study allows us to identify quantitative predictors of belonging, while also gaining a deeper understanding of the lived experience of organizational culture that impacts belonging among clinical support staff.  Together, these findings can inform a targeted organizational strategy.

column4: Emergency Department Utilization Amongst Patients Considered High Risk for Lung Cancer at UAB
column5:
Introduction:

In the United States, despite substantial healthcare expenditures, outcomes are disproportionately lower other high-income countries. A contributing factor is the misuse of emergency departments (ED), with respiratory symptoms being a frequent cause of such visits. Lung cancer patients, particularly minorities and low-income individuals, exhibit higher ED utilization, leading to poorer outcomes. Disparities in healthcare, notably related to race and insurance, contribute to these many negative health outcomes. The aim of this study is to delineate the sociodemographic traits of individuals at high risk for lung cancer and their utilization of the ED.

Methods:

A retrospective medical chart review at the University of Alabama at Birmingham Hospital (UAB) between 2015 and 2020, funded by NIH U54, focused on high-risk lung cancer patients undergoing low-dose CT screening. Participants aged 55-80, without recent lung cancer diagnostic codes, and with a smoking history were included.

Results:

Regression analysis of 1,129 observations revealed a significant correlation between race and ED visits for lung cancer patients (P=0.001). Compared to the white population, Black patients were four times more likely to visit the ED. Increasing age correlated with fewer ED visits, while being in a relationship and residing in a rural county were associated with decreased and increased ED visits, respectively.

Conclusion:

The study, limited to UAB's high-risk lung cancer patients, highlights the urgency of addressing healthcare disparities. While acknowledging data limitations, the methodology's applicability to different cancer diagnoses or chronic conditions suggests the potential for educational interventions to reduce unnecessary ED visits and enhance outcomes.
column6:
Impact: The study reveals a significant correlation between race and emergency department (ED) visits for lung cancer patients, particularly highlighting that Black patients are four times more likely than their white counterparts to seek ED care. 

Significance: Despite limitations, the research methodology can be extended to patients with various cancer diagnoses or chronic conditions, offering a background for interventions to reduce unnecessary ED visits and improve overall healthcare outcomes, emphasizing the broader relevance of addressing healthcare disparities.

column4: The Impact of Social Determinants of Health and Social History on Mental Health Patient Return to Emergency Departments: An Explainable AI Framework
column5:
Introduction: The return of mental health patients to emergency departments (EDs) within a month of being discharged poses a substantial challenge to healthcare systems and healthcare providers. To develop effective interventions, it is important to understand the factors that contribute to return. This research investigates the relationship between social determinants of health (SDoH) and social history and the risk of return to ED among mental health patients. 
Method: We retrospectively analyzed data from 300,000 mental health patients who visited the UABHS ED between 2018 and 2022. Our dataset included patient demographics, mental health diagnoses, two SDoH, and three social history factors: social support, alcohol/tobacco use, substance abuse, diet, and socioeconomic status. To predict readmission within 30 days, we developed XAI model using an XGBoost algorithm. The model development includes three stages: processing text data using advanced natural language processing (NLP) technique, feature selection, and model training and testing. Five measures are used to evaluate the proposed models: the area under the curve(AUC), accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and f1 score. Additionally, SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) is utilized to explain the features that have the most significant influence on the model’s predictions. 
Discussion/Results: This work is under development and will be ready by the time the poster is presented. Our analysis will reveal that several key predictors are associated with an increased risk of return within 30 days. 
Significance: This research provides a comprehensive explanation of how the clinical, SDoH, and social history factors affect mental health patients' return to ED within 30 days. It also provides an accurate risk score per patient. This allows social workers to identify patients at high risk of ED visit return and then develop strategies to minimize their return.
column6:
This research provides a comprehensive explanation of how the clinical, SDoH, and social history factors affect mental health patients' return to ED within 30 days. 
This allows social workers to identify patients at high risk of ED visit return and then develop strategies to minimize their return.

column4: An exploratory analysis of tele-wellness program in people with chronic respiratory disease and mobility limitations
column5:
An exploratory analysis of tele-wellness program in people with chronic respiratory disease and mobility limitations
Pooja Arora1, Salma Aly 2, Abebaw M. Yohannes,3 James Rimmer4
1.	Rehabilitation Science PhD Program, School of Health Professions, The University of Alabama at Birmingham, National Center on Health, Physical Activity and Disability, Birmingham, AL, United States.
2.	Department of Family and Community Medicine, The University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham, AL, United States.
3.	School of Health Professions, The University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham, AL, United States.
4.	School of Health Professions, The University of Alabama at Birmingham, National Center on Health, Physical Activity and Disability, Birmingham, AL, United States.

Abstract 
Introduction: The aim of the study is to examine the effects of 8-week tele-wellness, mindfulness, exercise, and nutrition to optimize resilience (MENTOR) program on physical activity, physical and mental health, coping abilities and mindfulness in people with chronic respiratory diseases (CRDs) and mobility limitations (ML). 
Methods: A sample of 32 participants with ML and CRDs was analyzed for demographic characteristics and self-reported questionnaires: PROMIS global health including physical and mental health scale, Godin Leisure -Time Exercise Questionnaire (GLTEQ), Connor-Davidson Resilience Scale-10 item (CD-RISC) and Mindfulness Attention Awareness Scale (MAAS). The outcomes measures were completed at baseline and at end of 8 weeks MENTOR program.
Results/Discussion: 32 participants were enrolled (mean age 54.5 ± 13.1[SD]. The PROMIS global physical health score at baseline showed that 87.6% of participants reported having poor to fair physical health and 8-weeks post-program, the percentage significantly decreased to 62%. The PROMIS global mental health scores showed that the percentage of participants with good to excellent health significantly increased from 64.6% to 70.9% (for baseline and post-program, respectively). 
The median GLTEQ score increased from 14.5 (0.0, 54.8) to 28.5 (2.3, 90.0) from baseline to post assessment with no statistically significant improvement (p=0.254). The participants showed minor increase in resilience measured by CD-RISC score from 26.7 + 6.8 to 27.4 + 8.1 post assessment with no statistically significant improvement (p=0.503), the MAAS scores showed no statistically significant improvement with a decrease in score at post assessment from 4.2 + 1.0 to 4.0 + 1.1 (p= 0.266). 
Significance: The MENTOR tele-wellness program is a promising intervention for people with CRDs. It showed trend of improvement in some of the outcome measures such as physical health, mental health, and leisure activities. However, further work is needed with the large sample size.
column6:
1) The MENTOR program is free, online program that could be accessed by large number of people with COPD in the community 
2) This 8 weeks program includes multidimensional components of wellness and thus address the varied pulmonary and non pulmonary manifestations of COPD and its associated co-morbidities.

column4: Calm Down: How to Improve Undergraduate Student Performance
column5: In recent decades, undergraduate students are progressively experiencing more stress. It is reported that stress negatively affects students' academic achievement; however, the connection between the two is still unclear. There are a multitude of both perceived and physiological factors that can influence stress levels, such as depression, anxiety, and coping skills. The objective of our study was to identify these factors and their influences on undergraduate student stress and GPA. The study was conducted in a renowned, research-heavy university in the Deep South with a primarily white population. Participants were interviewed using validated tools such as the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS), Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ), and the Brief COPE test at the start of the 2021 fall semester to assess perceived stress levels. Student GPA was also collected as a self-reported variable. Increased anxiety and depression were significantly correlated with increased perceived stress. Coping skills such as self-distraction, denial, behavioral disengagement, venting, and religion positively affected perceived stress, while active, instrumental support and acceptance decreased perceived stress. Student GPA was negatively modified only by those that practiced venting as a coping mechanism. University-reported GPA, rather than self-reported, should be used in future analysis. Further studies should be conducted to identify the most significant sources of increasing undergraduate student stress, specifically regarding students’ coping skills.
column6: - Identification of negative and positive coping mechanisms can aid students and mentors in developing coping mechanisms that are most beneficial for student mental health and academic performance.

column4: Racial Differences in Undergraduate Student Stress
column5:
Introduction: Undergraduate students, particularly undergraduates in the (Science Technology Engineering and Mathematics) STEM fields, are under immense pressure to succeed. This high pressure continued as these students move into their chosen career and has led to high burnout rates. Additionally racial microaggressions cause more stress and lead to increased attrition from STEM fields for Black students and workers in the STEM fields.

Methods: This study focuses on differences in stress, depression and anxiety using the validates tools Perceived Stress Scale (PSS), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ9) and General Anxiety Disorder (GAD7) respectively, among undergraduate Black and White STEM students. To assess the physiological response to stress, nail samples were collected and analyzed for cortisol the stress hormone.

Results/Discussion: Our results show that Black students are under higher levels of stress, depression, and anxiety than White undergraduate students. Black students have significantly higher coping skills than White students. There was no significant difference in cortisol levels between Black and White students, and there was no correlation between PSS and cortisol levels. These results indicate that despite differences in stress, anxiety and depression, there is no physiological differences in the expression of stress in Black and White students.

Significance: It is essential that we adequately prepare our students to handle psychological stress to better prepare them for future careers in STEM. Further, improving coping skills and stress management techniques can help to increase diversity in STEM, by increasing retention of racial minorities.
column6: - Improving coping skills and stress management techniques among STEM students will allow them to better handle psychological stress for their future careers as well as increase the retention of racial minorities in STEM fields.

column4: Improving Stress Management in Undergraduates Through Interactive Workshops
column5: Stress levels in college students is an important issue and the need for research on the effects of stress in students from different racial and ethnic backgrounds grows. Furthermore, undergraduate students should be given the opportunity to learn about how to handle stress and hardship in order to be prepared for future studies in graduate school and for their future careers. This research aims to measure the amount of stress in racial and ethnic groups of undergraduate students through the use of physiological tests, which will determine biological markers of stress in a variety of different samples, and psychological tests, which will aim to measure overall psychological well-being. Physiological tests will consist of urine, nails, feces, blood, and saliva samples while psychological tests will measure sleep quality, anxiety, depression, healthy eating, coping, stress, and resilience. This research aims to identify stress levels in a diverse group of students and to better understand coping skills and their impact on resilience and stress management. Additionally, seminars will be conducted to educate students on the importance of stress management and the implications of stress on both physical and mental health. These seminars will also work towards providing students with the skills and information to be self-sufficient in coping with stress and to improve resilience and grit. Long term follow-up of student stress and resilience will continue to be collected. This semester, students have been training on how to collect data and process samples. Sample collection and seminars will begin Fall 2024.
column6: Hope to understand the effects of stress

column4: Do Racially Diverse Teams Improve Student Learning?
column5:
Introduction: Diverse teams have higher cohesiveness, allow members to develop transferable skills and improve academic outcomes overall. While diversity improves team outcomes, it is unknown whether the racial make-up of a team impact individual student learning.  
Methods: To study the effects of team racial composition, randomly assigned teams from two Biomedical Sciences undergraduate courses were measured via CATME for overall grades and team effectiveness.  Data was analyzed by 1) if there was one member of a race on a team and 2) two or more members of the same race on the team. 
Results/Discussion: Our results show that grades are not affected by number of students of the same race for Black students, but team cohesiveness was improved when there was more than one Black student on a team. While individual grade increased with number of White students on the same team, there was no difference in team cohesiveness based on the number of White students on the team. Both average student grade and team cohesion increased with higher number of Asian students on the same team. This data highlights that those students of different races experience working and learning in diverse teams differently.
column6: Significance: While it is essential for students to learn how to work in diverse groups, this should not come at the cost of student learning. When creating teams in courses, ensuring at least two students of the same race are on the same team can improve both student learning and overall team effectiveness for many students.

column4: The Noah Project
column5:
The Noah Project is a collaborative and interdisciplinary effort pioneering a pathway to
directly influence those impacted by this disorder. There are three rare genetic disorders that our team is focused on: Bohring-Opitz Syndrome (ASXL1), Shashi-Pena Syndrome (ASXL2), and Bainbridge-Ropers Syndrome (ASXL3). Due to its rarity, healthcare professionals know very little about the disorder or how to treat it. Furthermore, only a small number of ASXL-related websites are available to the public. After conducting a SWOT analysis on these websites, our team decided that each of them had room for improvement, and making revisions to the most outstanding website would be more beneficial. In order to effectively decide which elements to add or remove, we created a survey that would be given to the families who have an impact. The team has begun the process of fundraising for the 2024 ASXL Family Conference and the Miracle League. Currently, the team is in the early stages of brainstorming community events, which include but are not limited to interactions with campus-based programming and non-profits within the greater Birmingham area. For the Spring 2024 semester, the team will begin a protocol to screen cell lines that will be used to test drugs in the ASXL mutation and wild type. In conclusion, The Noah Project aims to raise awareness and improve resources for families impacted by ASXL.
column6: .Clinical research can bring awareness for a plethora of children and adults with rare genetic disorders.

column4: The Motivations and Methods Behind Sharing a Pediatric Prader-Willi Syndrome Diagnosis
column5:
Introduction
Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) is a rare genetic condition with a diverse phenotype. Sharing information about the diagnosis with the affected child can be challenging due to its early age of onset, variability, and cognitive phenotype. While there is literature addressing the challenges of disclosing genetic conditions to children, no research specific to PWS exists. This study aims to evaluate how parents have shared information about a PWS diagnosis with their child and to examine motivations and influencing factors behind disclosure. 

Methods
Parents of children aged at least 5 years with a diagnosis of PWS completed a survey, and interview invitations were offered to a subset of parents who fully disclosed, partially disclosed, or did not disclose the diagnosis to their child. A total of 51 surveys and 15 interviews were completed.

Results/Discussion
The majority of parents (90%) shared their child’s health differences or symptoms with them to some extent. Parents were more likely to disclose if their child was older (p=0.02), had at least 1 sibling (p=0.046), and if the parent felt more knowledgeable about PWS (p=0.004). Interview themes demonstrated that parents had some similar motivations for disclosure, experiences with disclosure conversations, and methods to approach disclosure conversations. 

Significance
Healthcare providers and genetic counselors can play an integral role in addressing many of the disclosure needs expressed by parents. This research compiles parents’ motivations for disclosure, and overall strategies and experiences, providing information and validation for others considering disclosure of PWS or other rare genetic diagnoses to their child.
column6:
- This research provides information and emotional validation for parents of the rare disease community, as rare genetic conditions and the nuances of disclosing them are not well-researched.
- This research provides genetic counselors and other healthcare professionals that interact with the rare disease community information about how parents approach disclosure and what needs parents have in terms of support and education.

column4: Qualitatively assessing diverse perspectives regarding the benefits of art in medicine
column5:
Background: The observation and/or creation of art has shown increasing benefits in the development of empathy, stress reduction, and observation skills. Hypothesis: Incorporating art into medical practice can provide emotional relief while improving the clinical skills of physicians.
Methods/Results: Qualitative interviews will be conducted with UAB professors, residents, and artists regarding their demographics and current experiences with art using open-ended questions. The qualitative data will be coded in order to identify consensus themes from
respondents. We will conduct appropriate statistical analyses (i.e., t-tests, chi-square tests, ANOVA) of the data collected from the surveys and interviews.
Discussion/conclusion: These results are intended to make a novel contribution from the perspectives of healthcare professionals and artists regarding how the appreciation and/or creation of art can increase empathy, cultural awareness, perception skills, and enhance the overall well-being of physicians.
column6: The results are intended to make a novel contribution from the perspectives of healthcare professionals and artists regarding how the appreciation and/or creation of art can increase empathy, cultural awareness, perception skills, and enhance the overall well-being of physicians.

column5:
Background/Significance- Service learning is often overlooked as a viable method of research; however, its impact is far-reaching. Service learning plays a significant role in education by merging community service, real-world learning, and personal growth. Its impact extends to community organizations, students, and society as a whole.

Hypothesis:- Through hosting different events and fundraising throughout the Birmingham community, we aim to eliminate disparities while aiding first-year students in this pivotal year of their college experience.

Methods/results: This study sought to address disparities and supported first-year college students through various events and fundraisers in the Birmingham community. This study partnered with six freshmen groups (e.g. Firebreathers, Green and Gold Dragons, and SpitFire) and collaborated with community partners such as Ronald McDonald House, Firehouse Ministries, and First Light. Notably, the combined groups raised over $2000 

Discussion/Conclusion- Throughout mentorship with freshman groups and their involvement with the community, we have positively impacted the Birmingham community. Students engaged in these projects develop industry-specific skills, gain insights into workplace dynamics, and expand their professional networks. Academic growth is also evident, fostering critical thinking and problem-solving abilities. Moving forward, we will maintain communication with our community partners and assess the impact of the freshman group’s service projects. Challenges such as improved team communication and time constraints for the freshman groups will be addressed to enhance future initiatives.
column6: Throughout mentorship with freshman groups and their involvement with the community, we have positively impacted the Birmingham community. Students engaged in these projects develop industry-specific skills, gain insights into workplace dynamics, and expand their professional networks. Academic growth is also evident, fostering critical thinking and problem-solving abilities.

column4: Examining the Extent of Diversity Signaling via Healthcare Organization Websites
column5:
Background: Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) have become central to corporate practices, especially in the healthcare industry. The study employs signaling theory to explore how healthcare organizations convey their commitment to DEI through their websites to external stakeholders. Signaling theory posits that organizations use signals to communicate information to stakeholders, and in healthcare, this information is crucial for prospective employees and patients. The corporate website, as a primary communication channel, serves as a vital tool for conveying organizational values, including DEI commitments.
Methods: The study analyzes a nationally representative sample of 78 hospitals and healthcare organizations, examining their websites for signals related to DEI. The signaling theory framework helps evaluate the extent of diversity signaling, considering variables such as gender and ethnic diversity in leadership, explicit diversity language in mission/vision/values, and the presence of diversity statements. A composite measure scale is developed to quantify the magnitude of diversity signaling.
Results: Quantitative analysis found while 88.5% of organizations exhibit observable C-Suite diversity (gender or ethnic diversity), explicit diversity language in core statements is less common (10.3%). Most organizations (78.2%) lack explicit diversity language in strategic statements, with organizational value statements being the primary location for such language. The composite score reveals a varied commitment to diversity signaling, with most organizations scoring low (49% with a score of 1) and some displaying no diversity signaling strategies (10%). 
Significance: The potential implications of the absence of diversity signals, suggests a possible unintended projection of a lack of commitment to DEI. The study emphasizes the impact on both potential employees and patients, as the absence of diversity signals may dissuade those seeking inclusive environments. The examination of C-Suite diversity underscores its role in shaping organizational culture and its potential influence on health equity efforts. Organizations who actively communicate their commitment to DEI through explicit language and diverse leadership are better positioned to attract talent and create inclusive environments.

Keywords: Signaling theory, websites, DEI, hospitals
column6: Organizations who actively communicate their commitment to DEI through explicit language and diverse leadership are better positioned to attract talent and create inclusive environments.

column4: Bioenhancing Assessment of Black Pepper with Turmeric on Self-Reported Pain Ratings: A Randomized, Crossover Clinical Trial
column5:
Introduction: Chronic pain that lasts 3 months or longer is estimated to affect 51 million US adults. Those with chronic pain do not always tolerate pharmaceutical therapies. Integrative or alternative approaches may be safe and effective tools to combat chronic pain. Turmeric has been shown to exhibit pain relieving and anti-inflammatory properties, albeit with limited bioavailability. Black pepper is also shown to have bioenhancing effects, increasing the bioavailability or bioactive effectiveness of other dietary components. Black pepper may enhance dietary levels of turmeric to decrease pain in adults. 

Methods: A randomized, crossover clinical trial (NCT05206266) tested the effectiveness of turmeric only or turmeric with black pepper to reduce pain in adults aged 40 years and older. Participants (n=30, with moderate pain: 4–7 on 10-point scale) were enrolled in a 21-day trial and randomized into receiving one of three different amounts of turmeric (3 grams, 1 gram, or 300 mg) and two sequenced treatment periods: a 7-day treatment period (turmeric with or without black pepper), a 7-day washout period, and a 7-day treatment period (turmeric with or without black pepper). Participants were blinded to treatment assignment (via encapsulation of the spices) and, using an experience sampling methodology approach, were prompted to report current pain using the numeric pain rating scale (NPRS; 0-10) via text message three times per day for the full study period. Analysis of covariance (ANOVA) was used to analyze differences between turmeric amount and sequenced treatment groups.  

Results/Discussion: A total of n=29 participants (completers) were included in the analysis. Data collected included age (54.3±6.8 years), sex (predominately female, 93.10%), BMI (35.3±5.5), baseline pain ratings (4.3±2.2), washout period pain ratings (3.0±2.1), turmeric only pain ratings (3.0±1.9), and turmeric with black pepper (3.0±2.1) pain ratings. Results show significant differences in treatment periods and baseline for reported pain (p=<0.0001). Turmeric amounts and pain ratings are statistically different between groups (p=<0.0001). Turmeric at culinary amounts with or without black pepper may reduce pain in older adults. 

Significance: Providing a low cost, easy to implement, and generally well tolerated option for pain relief may help improve quality of life in those suffering from chronic pain.
column6:
-Could help improve Activities of Daily Living in adults with chronic pain
-Could provide more options for pain treatment in those suffering moderate pain

column4: Post-Challenge Insulin Responses as Predictors of Fat Loss
column5:
Introduction: Prior research has shown that insulin responses from oral glucose tolerance tests can be used to predict which diet an individual will lose more weight on. Therefore, we investigated insulin responses from different metabolic tests as predictors of diet-induced fat loss. We hypothesized 2 insulin response indices (AIRg and insulin-30) would predict fat loss in participants on a reduced carbohydrate diet, but not a standard carbohydrate diet.

Methods: This was a secondary analysis of a weight loss clinical trial where all food was provided during the study. Adults with obesity were assigned to either a reduced carbohydrate (43% carbohydrate) or standard carbohydrate (55% carbohydrate) diet for 16 weeks. AIRg from an intravenous glucose tolerance test and insulin-30 from a liquid meal tolerance test were collected at baseline. The outcome was 16-week fat mass loss and was measured using dual energy x-ray absorptiometry at baseline and follow-up. Multiple linear regression models were used to test the relationship between the insulin response and fat loss, adjusting for BMI, diet, and insulin sensitivity.

Results/Discussion: 
Higher AIRg at baseline was associated with greater 16-week fat loss. When testing this relationship by diet group, AIRg was associated with fat mass loss only in the reduced carbohydrate group. Insulin-30 did not predict fat mass loss in any model. More research is needed as the observation cannot solely attributed to AIRg.
 
Significance: This is the first study to show AIRg predicts fat mass loss during a diet intervention. This is relevant for prospective personalized nutrition trials.
column6: This study contributed to the understanding of whether pretreatment insulin variables are pertinent for predicting obesity outcomes in a diverse population.

column4: Household disorder, meal timing, and cardiometabolic biomarkers of children 4 to 10 years old
column5:
Introduction: Household disorder is associated with family meal patterns, eating behaviors, and obesity risk in children. Previous research in adults has suggested later meal timing, characterized by food intake late in the day, is adversely associated with biomarkers such as leptin, adiponectin, and HOMA-IR. However, little is known about whether household disorder is related to children’s meal timing, and if time of last daily food intake is adversely associated with cardiometabolic biomarkers.

Objective: This study investigated the relationships between 1) household disorder and meal timing, 2) meal timing and biomarkers, and 3) household disorder and biomarkers among children 4 to 10 years old.

Methods: Household disorder was assessed using the Confusion, Hubbub and Order Scale. Meal timing was collected via 24-hour dietary recalls and defined as the average time of child’s last food intake for the day. A fasted blood draw measured glucose, insulin, leptin, and adiponectin. HOMA-IR was derived from glucose and insulin. Linear regressions tested the relationships between household disorder, children’s meal timing, and biomarkers. 

Results/Discussion: Children (n = 182) were 53% female and 7.11 ± 2.06 years old. Household disorder was not associated with average time of child’s last food intake, fasting leptin, adiponectin or HOMA-IR. Similarly, children’s meal timing was not predictive of biomarkers.

Significance: Further investigation is needed to evaluate the relationship between household disorder and obesity risk in children; factors for future consideration include children’s sleep habits and diet quality, and caregiver feeding practices.
column6: Although previous research has demonstrated an adverse relationship between household disorder and obesity risk in children, household disorder may not be associated with children's cardiometabolic biomarkers or late night eating.

column4: Behavior-Based Identities Are Important in Weight Loss and Maintenance: Findings from Three Studies Using Data from IWCR
column5:
Introduction: Identifying as someone who regularly engages in health promoting behaviors (i.e., behavior-based identities) may be associated with maintenance of behaviors such as healthy eating and exercising that are important for sustained weight loss. Three studies were conducted in a large sample of adults participating in the International Weight Control Registry (IWCR) to validate the psychometric properties of a four-item exercise identity measure (4-EI) and the adapted healthy eater identity measure (4-HEI) and investigate the role of 4-EI and 4-HEI in weight management.   

Methods: Data were collected via online surveys from December 2020 through October 2021. In Study 1 (n=839), exploratory factor analysis was used to assess the proposed unidimensional factor structure of the 4-EI and 4-HEI, and Spearman rank-order correlations was used to examine convergent and discriminant validity. In Study 2 (n=870), univariate analysis of variance was used to compare 4-EI and 4-HEI scores (-3 to +3, higher score=stronger identity) across three self-defined weight loss categories (Successful, Regain, and Unsuccessful) and those who have or have not maintained ≥ 5% weight loss for ≥ 1 year. In Study 3 (n=1283), two mediation analysis models were used to test whether eating and exercise behaviors mediated the associations between selected socioecological factors (environment, social support, and identity) and weight change during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Results: In Study 1, the analyses supported the internal reliability, and structural, convergent and discriminant validities of the 4-EI and 4-HEI. In Study 2, stronger 4-EI and 4-HEI were observed in participants who self-defined as Successful compared to Regain (4-EI: -0.18±1.68 [Mean ± SD]; 4-HEI: 0.57±1.48) and Unsuccessful (4-EI: -0.28±1.62; 4-HEI: 0.51±1.33), and among those who have maintained ≥ 5% weight loss for ≥ 1 year (4-EI: 0.47±0.78; 4-HEI: 1.13±1.49) compared to those who have not (4-EI: -0.27±1.66; 4-HEI: 0.53±1.47), p’s < 0.001. In Study 3, 4-EI and 4-HEI had the strongest association of the socioecological factors examined with weight change during the COVID-19 pandemic and the associations were mediated through healthy eating and exercise behaviors.

Significance: The findings suggest that behavior-based identities are differentiating factors in those successfully maintaining weight loss and promote resilience for sustaining healthy behaviors under challenging circumstances. Future intervention studies should implement strategies to improve behavior-based identities during behavior change.
column6:
1. The findings support the importance of behavior-based identity in long-term WL maintenance.

2. The findings support that stronger behavior-based identity help individuals to maintain better adherence to health-promoting behaviors and body weight goals during challenging situation.

column4: Lifestyle Wellness: A Novel and Comprehensive Wellness Assessment for Lifestyle-based Interventions
column5:
While weight status and clinical laboratory measures are important in assessing obesity-related disease severity and chronic disease risk, including a broader range of emotional, psychosocial, and behavioral factors would provide greater context of an individual’s overall state of wellness and could be used to better guide treatment decisions. The purpose of this research was to develop a comprehensive Lifestyle Wellness assessment for use in lifestyle-based wellness interventions and programs. 
An exploratory factor analysis (EFA) was conducted using baseline data from N=138 adults who were participating in behavioral weight loss trials. An unweighted least squares extraction method with oblique rotation was used. Twenty-four putative measures spanning constructs related to metabolic health, emotional health/wellbeing, body weight and composition, diet quality, and fitness were analyzed for retention in the Lifestyle Wellness assessment. 

Mean BMI was 38.0 ± 6.6 kg/m2, mean age was 57.3 years, and 77.5% of participants were female. The EFA produced a five-factor model with 13 items that explained 80.3% of the variance. The retained factors included: 1) Emotional Health: mindfulness, resilience, quality of life, and happiness; 2) Metabolic Health – Blood Pressure: systolic and diastolic blood pressure, 3) Metabolic Health – Lipids:  total cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol, 4) Body State: BMI and waist circumference, and 5) Fitness State: grip strength, jump height, and percent body fat. 
These findings show that anthropometric assessments of “body state” are distinguishable from metabolic health and body fat clustered with fitness-related measures in adults with obesity. Thus, Lifestyle Wellness is a more comprehensive assessment that enables innovative wellness-related research such as metabolically health obesity and weight-neutral interventions, and should be investigated in additional populations with greater age, sex/gender, and body size diversity.
column6:
1. This is a novel and comprehensive wellness assessment for lifestyle-based interventions.

2. The Lifestyle Wellness Assessment was tested in people with obesity and can be used as a tool for obesity treatment.

column4: Nutrition Recommendations for Adults with Spinal Cord Injury: A Delphi Study
column5:
Introduction
This Delphi study aimed to establish a consensus-based framework for addressing the nutritional needs of adults with chronic spinal cord injury (SCI) who can ingest food orally and live in non-clinical settings. The Delphi panel consisted of experts knowledgeable in SCI and nutrition, including physicians, registered dietitians (RDs), and researchers.
Methods
A computer-mediated real-time Delphi design was employed to ascertain the consensus and agreement of the experts. Informed by a literature review, 18 nutrition recommendations tailored for adults with SCI were proposed to be rated by the 15 panelists. The survey included statements about SCI-specific dietary energy assessments and macro and micronutrients. Ratings and consensus details were outlined for each recommendation. The Kruskal-Wallis test was also employed to assess rating differences among the panel's professional groups. 
Results
The response rate for the Delphi panel (N=15) was 100  %. Consensus levels, scores, stability levels, and response numbers were documented for each recommendation. Alternative expert comments and suggestions were also provided for each recommendation to enhance understanding. The Kruskal-Wallis test indicated significant differences in the ratings among the professional groups for only two recommendations, suggesting an overall agreement in  the majority of the nutrition recommendations.
Significance
Engaging a diverse panel of experts, including physicians, RDs, and researchers, the real-time Delphi process yielded consensus-based nutrition recommendations rooted in a literature review and expert opinions. The presented results contribute to the ongoing dialogue in SCI-specific nutrition, providing a practical and expert-rated resource for healthcare professionals dealing with adults with chronic SCI.
column6: The presented results contribute to the ongoing dialogue in SCI-specific nutrition, providing a practical and expert-rated resource for healthcare professionals dealing with adults with chronic SCI.

column4: Moderators of a Web-based Lifestyle Intervention on Diet, Physical Activity, and Weight in Cancer Survivors and their Partners: The DUET Trial
column5:
Purpose: To identify who benefits most from web-based lifestyle interventions, this study examined potential moderators of the DUET web-based lifestyle intervention on diet quality, moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA), and weight.
Methods: Baseline and 6-month data from 112 cancer survivors and their partners enrolled in the DUET intervention were used. Web-based surveys captured data on potential moderators and were explored using Mplus software. Diet quality and caloric intake were measured through 2-day dietary recalls, MVPA via GLTEQ and accelerometry, and weight on a digital scale.
Results: The sample was 76.8% female, 62.5% non-Hispanic White and had a mean age of 58 years. The DUET intervention showed similar effects across genders and varying levels of comorbidity and eHEALTH literacy, with limited moderating factors identified. However, analyses indicated that race/ethnicity moderated the intervention's impact on caloric intake, with non-Hispanic Whites experiencing greater reductions in calories compared to non-Hispanic Blacks and others (interaction effect (b3) = -29.97, 95% CI [-58.296, -4.304]). Education moderated the effect of the intervention on weight, with college educated participants achieving greater weight loss compared to less educated individuals (b3 = -1.60, 95% CI [-3.036, -0.450]). Lastly, depression moderated the intervention's effect on self-reported MVPA, indicating that individuals with higher depressive symptoms showed less improvement in self-reported MVPA compared to those with lower depressive symptoms (b3 = -0.20, 95% CI [-0.332, -0.029]).
Significance: These findings call for continued research to better engage those with lower levels of education and minoritized populations, while also recognizing that depression may require ancillary treatment.
column6: These findings are impactful as it highlights disparities in the effectiveness of a web-based weight loss intervention delivered among cancer survivors and their partners. The greater impact observed among non-Hispanic White individuals, those with a college education, and those with lower levels of depression suggests that intervention outcomes may not be equally beneficial across diverse demographic and mental health profiles. Understanding these disparities is crucial for tailoring future web-based lifestyle interventions for cancer survivors to address specific needs and ensuring more equitable health behavior outcomes for all populations.

column4: Timing of energy intake as a possible intervention target among postpartum people
column5:
Introduction: Following pregnancy, most people fail to return to their pre-pregnancy weight, which contributes to increased risk for obesity and cardiovascular disease. Due to infant-related sleep disruptions, late-night eating may be more common postpartum, which has been speculated to contribute to postpartum weight retention. While meal timing may be a relevant intervention target postpartum, no studies have characterized the timing of intake or willingness to engage in time-based eating approaches postpartum. We present preliminary findings to begin addressing this gap.

Methods: Preliminary data from an ongoing study recruiting people who are 5-24 weeks postpartum from UAB hospital were used. Participants completed demographic and meal timing suveys. Participants also ranked their preferences for various timing-based eating approaches shown to have health benefits.

Results/Discussion: Participants (n=17) were 31.53 ± 5.32 years old and majority non-Hispanic White. The average time of first and last caloric intake on a workday was 06:47 and 20:55, respectively. On free days, first and last caloric intake were 07:47 and 19:46, respectively. Majority preferred a bedtime stopping rule approach and were willing to stop eating 2.2 ± 1.1 hours before bedtime.

Significance: Postpartum people may be eating over a longer daily period compared to the average American eating window of 12 hours. These preliminary data suggest postpartum people may be willing to follow a timing-based dietary intervention, preferring a bedtime stopping rule. Using the complete sample (N=55), results will be validated using 24-hour diet recalls and we will examine associations of meal timing with weight and body composition.
column6:
-Meal timing-based approaches show promise for weight management and improving cardiometabolic health in non-postpartum samples but have not been considered as a potential intervention approach postpartum.

-This study will provide necessary formative data to inform the development of a novel meal timing-based intervention adapted for postpartum people that has the potential to improve a birthing persons long-term health trajectory.

column4: Screening for Eating Disorders in Adolescents and Young Adults with Spina Bifida
column5:
Introduction: Prior research has identified an increased risk of obesity in spina bifida (SB) patients. Because of this, efforts have focused on reducing obesity in this population; however, the prevalence of eating disorder pathology in adolescents and young adults (AYA) with SB is unclear.

Methods: AYA with SB (ages 15-30) were asked to complete the Eating Disorder Examination Questionnaire (EDE-Q). Descriptive statistics identified the prevalence of eating disorder pathology and one-sample t-tests compared SB EDE-Q scores to population means. Obese SB patients were identified and Mann-Whitney U tests compared EDE-Q scores for these SB patients to non-obese SB patients.

Results/Discussion: Thirty-six participants with SB (mean age 21) completed the EDE-Q. Our spina bifida cohort did not significantly differ from general AYA population norms; however, when assessing questions that asked about presence and frequency of eating disorder behaviors, 46% of SB respondents met DSM criteria for an eating disorder even though none of these participants had been identified as having an eating disorder before.

When comparing obese SB patients to non-obese Individuals, those categorized as obese had significantly higher scores in the global, eating concern, shape concern, and weight concern subscales of the EDE-Q.

Significance: These results suggest that eating disorders are present in AYA with SB and hyper-focusing on preventing obesity may be detrimental. Due to the high morbidity associated with eating disorders, it is critical for all physicians working with this population to be familiar with eating disorder symptomatology to expeditiously address pathology, particularly in high-risk populations.
column6:
-identifying gaps in research regarding minority groups
-advocating in determinants of health research

column4: ADAPTIVE THERMOGENESIS AT THE LEVEL OF RESTING ENERGY EXPENDITURE AFTER DIET ALONE OR DIET PLUS BARIATRIC SURGERY
column5:
Introduction: Weight loss is followed by adaptive thermogenesis (AT), and increased drive to eat. The impact of weight loss modality on AT remains controversial and a potential association between AT and changes in appetite has not been explored. This study aimed to compare AT after diet alone or diet plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) or sleeve gastrectomy (SG) and to investigate if AT was associated with appetite changes.
Methods: 44 participants with severe obesity underwent 10 weeks of a very-low energy diet alone or in combination with RYGB, or SG. Body weight, fat mass (FM), fat-free mass (FFM), resting metabolic rate (RMR), subjective appetite feelings, and plasma concentrations of gastrointestinal hormones were measured at baseline and week 11 (when participants were ketotic). AT was defined as a significantly lower measured versus predicted (using a regression model with baseline data) RMR.
Results: Participants lost on average 18.4±3.9 kg body weight, 13.5±3.2 kg FM, and 4.8±2.2 kg FFM (p < 0.001 for all), and experienced AT (-121±188 kcal/day, p < 0.001), with no differences among groups. The larger the AT, the greater the reduction (or smaller the increase) in fasting ghrelin (r=0.337, p=0.029), and the smaller the reduction (or larger the increase) in postprandial feelings of hunger (r=0.332, p=0.028), and desire to eat (r=0.396, p=0.008) over time. 
Significance: Weight loss modality does not seem to modulate the magnitude of AT. Those experiencing a greater AT had a smaller increase in ghrelin in fasting and reported a larger increase in desire to eat postprandially, under ketogenic conditions.
column6:
•	Bariatric surgery does not seem to impact AT, at the level of REE, beyond weight loss. 
•	Since individuals who experience a larger AT also feel hungrier in the postprandial state, they may initiate the following meal earlier and consume more food, therefore compromising diet adherence. Future studies should try to identify people at risk of experiencing a large AT, so that measures can be implemented aiming at improving dietary adherence and weight loss outcomes.

column4: Impact of Dietary Patterns on Cardiovascular Health in Women Post-Gestational Diabetes Mellitus: : A Mixed-Methods Study
column5:
Introduction: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) significantly impacts both immediate pregnancy outcomes and long-term health risks, including cardiovascular disease (CVD), the leading cause of death in women worldwide. Despite known associations between diet and CVD risk reduction in the general population, the impact of dietary patterns on women with a history of GDM remains underexplored.
Methods: This mixed-methods study utilizes data from the REGARDS and HAPi studies, focusing on women with a history of GDM. It aims to evaluate the relationship between adherence to healthy dietary patterns (aMED, DASH, and aHEI) and cardiovascular health outcomes, including CVD incidence and biomarkers. The study also seeks to identify barriers and facilitators to adopting these dietary patterns among women post-GDM.
Results/Discussion: While specific results are pending, the study hypothesizes that adherence to healthful dietary patterns will be associated with fewer CVD events and more favorable biomarker profiles among women with a history of GDM. Additionally, it anticipates identifying significant personal, interpersonal, environmental, and societal factors influencing dietary behaviors in this population.
Significance: Understanding the impact of dietary patterns on CVD risk among women with a history of GDM could lead to tailored dietary recommendations, potentially reducing CVD incidence. This research may also influence dietary behaviors within families, offering broader public health benefits.
column6:
1. By examining dietary patterns and their link to cardiovascular disease (CVD) and biomarkers, we aim to formulate customized dietary guidelines to avert and diminish CVD risk.
2. Given the significant influence of women's dietary preferences on their families' eating habits, our study foresees a ripple effect, enhancing the cardiovascular health of whole households through informed dietary choices.

column4: Exploring the effects of weight loss on neuroinflammation in adults with multiple sclerosis (MS)
column5:
Introduction: Multiple sclerosis (MS) effects nearly 1 million people in the United States, and over half of this population also has overweight/obesity. Obesity is a significant risk factor for decreased physical function and increased disability and hospitalizations in adults with MS. Although the relationship between obesity and MS is not well understood, a leading theory is that the pro-inflammatory state of obesity and co-occurring cardiometabolic conditions may compound the inflammatory state of MS, including increasing neuroinflammation, which may speed progression of the disease. Dietary intervention research in MS is early, and limited research has explored if weight loss directly impacts brain inflammation in adults with MS. The purposes of this study are: 1. to determine if weight loss is associated with reduced inflammation, and 2. to explore differences in neuroinflammation based on adipose distribution. We hypothesize that weight loss will reduce overall inflammation and reductions in neuroinflammation will be more strongly associated with reductions in total fat mass than lean mass.  

Methods: The FOOD for MS trial is a randomized controlled trial in 100 adults with MS being conducted at UAB and Washington University in St. Louis (WUSTL). The primary aims of the trial are to test the efficacy of two dietary interventions, glycemic load and calorie restriction, to improve physical and cognitive function. As exploratory aims, we are also collecting variables related to inflammation, body composition, and neurodegeneration.  

Results/Significance: If these hypotheses are supported, the results could provide practical clinical weight loss and dietary recommendations for adults with MS.
column6: If these hypotheses are supported, the results could provide practical clinical weight loss and dietary recommendations for adults with MS.

column4: Unique Challenges of Recruitment and Enrollment of Postpartum Women into a Pilot Study
column5:
Introduction: Most women experience postpartum weight retention (PPWR) and existing weight management interventions have limited success postpartum. Due to infant-related sleep disruptions, postpartum women may be more likely to eat overnight, which has adverse effects on weight and metabolism. However, data characterizing eating patterns postpartum are scarce and no studies have considered meal timing as an intervention target for managing PPWR. Our objective is to describe recruitment and enrollment rates to-date for a parent study aimed at characterizing postpartum eating schedules and exploring factors impacting meal timing postpartum.  

Methods: Postpartum women (N=55) are being recruited. UAB Center for Women's Reproductive Health in-patient research staff screen medical records and approach potentially eligible participants in the postpartum unit. Study staff follow up with potential participants 5-24 weeks postpartum to confirm eligibility and schedule an enrollment visit. Participants who enroll are asked to complete surveys, undergo body measurements, and complete a qualitative interview to understand factors influencing eating patterns postpartum. 

Results/Discussion: From 05/2023 to 10/2023, 418 medical records were screened. Of these, 171 were potentially eligible/interested in the study and contacted at 5-24 weeks postpartum. In total, 20 participants have enrolled in the study. The majority of enrolled participants are White, married, and have at least a Bachelor’s degree. 

Significance: Given the challenges of new parenthood, recruitment during this period is challenging. An in-patient method of recruitment resulted in high refusal rates and relatively low enrollment. Thus, investigators have begun piloting a new recruitment method targeting patients at their postpartum care visit.
column6:
-The present project will provide important pilot data on effective recruitment strategies for engaging postpartum women in research studies.
-This will be the first study to characterize the timing of eating postpartum and how meal timing is associated with weight and body composition postpartum. Findings will be used to inform a novel timing-based intervention for postpartum weight management.

column4: Implications of Physical Activity During School Hours on Pediatric Metabolic Health
column5:
Title: Implications of Physical Activity During School Hours on Pediatric Metabolic Health
 
BACKGROUND: Seventeen percent of US children have obesity, placing them at risk for future health conditions (high blood pressure, stroke, and type 2 diabetes). Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) reduces risk for said conditions. Due to the large amount of time children are at school, this structured environment may constrain physical activity. Consequently, it is of interest whether activity during evening hours, specifically, is associated with biomarkers of metabolic health. OBJECTIVES: To evaluate the relationships of MVPA (total time, during school hours, out of school hours) with (A) insulin resistance and (B) leptin/adiponectin ratio (L/A) independent of current weight status. METHODS: Physical activity was assessed using waist-worn accelerometers and MVPA was calculated as the sum of moderate and vigorous physical activity. Activity was divided into school (7AM-3PM) and non-school hours (3:01PM-10PM). Fasting insulin, glucose, leptin, and adiponectin were measured to derive HOMA-IR and L/A ratio. Linear regression models explore relationships between MVPA and (A) HOMA-IR and (B) L/A ratio. PARTICIPANTS: N=226 participants between 4 and 11 years of age (6.94±2.04) will be considered in this secondary analysis. ANTICIPATED RESULTS: We hypothesize that MVPA will be negatively related with insulin resistance and L/A ratio, independent of children’s current weight status.
column6:
-To explore the benefits of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) on pediatric metabolic health 
-To evaluate the importance of physical activity in the evening in prevention of insulin resistance

column4: The association of carbohydrate intake in early pregnancy and infant adiposity differs for women with and without obesity
column5:
Introduction: Research examining the association of maternal carbohydrate intake and newborn adiposity has yielded inconsistent findings.  Given that glucose tolerance is lower for women with obesity, we sought to test the hypothesis that the association of carbohydrate intake and infant body fat (%) would be stronger for women with obesity versus normal weight in early pregnancy. 

Methods: Data from N=54 women enrolled in the Baby’s Early Environments (BEE) study were used in this analysis. Women were stratified by BMI at entry to prenatal care: normal weight (BMI of 18.5-24.9; N=29) and obese (BMI >29.9, N=25). A 3-day food diary was used to obtain dietary data and the Nutrition Data System for Research was used to derive average total 24-hour energy intake and % kcal from carbohydrate. Infant body composition was measured at 2-weeks of age via air displacement plethysmography. Simple Pearson Correlations and Partial Correlations were used to calculate associations of carbohydrate intake with infant % body fat.

Results: In the overall sample, maternal %kcal from carbohydrate was positively associated with infant % fat mass (r=0.472; p= 0.003). When split into groups, the correlation was only significant among the women with obesity (r=0.596; p=0.009 vs. r=0.422; p=0.064). The association for women with obesity remained after controlling for maternal total energy intake, infant sex, and age of infant and the body composition assessment (r=0.559; p= 0.030 vs. r=0.395; p=0.116).

Discussion/Significance: These data suggest that even among women with glucose tolerance, those with obesity may benefit from recommendations to limit carbohydrate intake during pregnancy.
